abstract: Bisphenol A (BPA) is a ubiquitous endocrine disruptor found in a wide array of consumer products. Recent legislative restrictions and public health concerns—highlighted by the European Food Safety Authority’s (EFSA) drastic reduction of the tolerable daily intake—have prompted the industrial sector to adopt structural analogues such as BPF, BPS, and BPAF. While these substitutes possess similar polymer-forming properties, their safety profiles remain poorly understood. This review systematically evaluates current research on the immunomodulatory effects of these emerging BPA alternatives. Evidence from in silico, in vitro, and in vivo studies indicates that these analogues can transition macrophages toward pro-inflammatory states, impair the maturation of dendritic cells, and alter T-lymphocyte cytokine profiles (specifically Th17 responses). Furthermore, exposure is linked to oxidative stress, genotoxicity in peripheral blood mononuclear cells, and significant disruptions to the gut microbiome and intestinal immunity. These findings suggest that BPA analogues are not inherently safer alternatives and may contribute to the development of immune-mediated pathologies, including allergies, asthma, and autoimmune disorders. The review concludes by identifying critical gaps in the literature, particularly regarding sex-specific responses and the cumulative effects of bisphenol mixtures.
